title: Combinatorial approaches as a component of high-throughput experimentation (HTE) in catalysis research
abstract:
Combinatorial Approaches As a
Component of High-Throughput
Experimentation (HTE) in
Catalysis Research
John M. Newsam,1 F. Schu¨ th2
1Molecular Simulations, Inc., 9685 Scranton Road,
San Diego, California 92121; e-mail: jmn@msi.com
2Max Planck Institute fu¨ r Kohlenforschung, Mu¨ lheim, Germany
Received 7 May 1999; accepted 20 June 1999
Abstract: We consider the application of high-
throughput experimentation (HTE), including com-
binatorial methods, to catalyst discovery and early-
phase optimization. While combinatorial- and par-
allel-testing methods have an already substantial
history in catalysis, recent work by several groups
promises significant efficiency gains. In molecular
catalysis, progress is noted in library design, library
synthesis by pooled, parallel, and discrete formats,
catalyst testing and reaction optimization; the
prime constraint for organometallic catalysts is the
limited scope of synthesis procedures for non-
peptide-based ligand libraries. Routes described
for the synthesis of heterogeneous catalysts in-
clude hydrothermal synthesis, arraying of solution
precursors, automated impregnation and precipita-
tion, and arraying of solid precursors. The key chal-
lenge in applying HTE to heterogeneous catalysis is
testing; we distinguish here between Stage 1, or
“discovery” testing and Stage 2, optimization test-
ing, and describe techniques with potential in each
case. Recent examples from the literature and our
own work are used to illustrate these principles and
the prospects for HTE applied to catalysis. © 1999
John Wiley & Sons, Inc. Biotechnol Bioeng (Comb Chem)
61:203–216, 1998/1999.
Keywords: catalysis; high-throughput experimen-
tation
INTRODUCTION
There are few fields where combinatorial chemistry
has had as long and as productive a history as cataly-
sis.1,2 Most catalysts that are in commercial use, both
homogeneous and heterogeneous, have been devel-
oped via some form of combinatorial approach, even if
the term has been applied only recently. A classic
example is the development of the ammonia-synthesis
catalyst by Mittasch at BASF, who ran some 6500
distinct experiments over a 3-year period, in evaluat-
ing various combinations of supports, catalysts, pro-
cessing, and reaction conditions.3 The conditions of
catalyst use are, in some cases, mild; Ziegler-Natta
polymerizations are typically run at 50–90°C and 0.8–
3.5 Mpa. Many reactions catalyzed heterogeneously,
however, are run at elevated temperatures and pres-
sures. In the Haber process explored by Mittasch, the
catalyst is run at 450–600°C and pressures of some
100 Mpa; automotive-exhaust catalysts that oxidize
CO to CO2, hydrocarbons to CO2 and H2O, and reduce
nitrogen oxides, NOx, to N2, operate between 300° and
650°C, and in fluid catalytic-cracking units, that are
the source of much of our gasoline, the zeolite catalyst
functions at some 700°C.
In the sense used in drug discovery, combinatorial
chemistry can contribute to catalyst synthesis, be it by
a pooled or parallel approach. However, synthesis is
only one component in a larger cycle that includes also
processing, characterization, and catalytic testing. It is
difficult to take other than a “system’s view” (Fig. 1)
in which catalyst performance represents the combi-
nation of a set of properties, these being determined by
structure at atomic and longer-length scales which, in
turn, is controlled by synthesis and processing condi-
tions. For this reason, the term “High-Throughout Ex-
perimentation” (HTE) is preferred. HTE for catalysis
represents, to some degrees, a logical continuation of
an ongoing industrial trend toward reduced reactor
volumes, concurrent testing, and computerized con-
trol. What today’s technology promises is much
greater efficiency, through miniaturization, automa-
tion, integration, and the effective use of computation.
Catalysis is a broad field encompassing biocatalysis
or, at least in the present context, chemical applica-
tions of biocatalysis; chemical catalysis, for the pro-
duction of bulk products such as ethylene oxide and
Correspondence to: John M. Newsam
© 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
CCC 0006-3592/98/040203-14
 specialties such as chiral pharmaceutical intermedi-
ates; refinery catalysis; polymerization catalysis; and
environmental catalysis. Catalysis is well-suited for an
HTE approach. First, it is a cornerstone of the chemi-
cal processing industry, with more than 80% of all
conversions, bulk or specialty, relying on a catalytic
process. The catalyst business itself is substantial,
some $9.7 billion in 1997, with contributions from the
chemical ($2.8 bn), polymerization ($1.5 bn), refinery
($2.4 bn) and environmental ($3.0 bn) areas;4 the eco-
nomic impact of a commercial catalyst improvement is
often 1 to 2 orders of magnitude greater than the value
of the catalyst itself. These economic aspects are im-
portant, given the relatively high-investment cost of an
HTE approach. Second, most catalysts in commercial
use today are the results of Edisonian approaches to
discovery and optimization; catalysis has been an al-
most purely empirical science. Third, the scope for
innovation and for further catalyst or process improve-
ment is considered large. There are many conversions
for which a suitable catalyst has yet to be found, such
as the efficient partial oxidation of methane or direct
conversion of propylene to propylene oxide. Several
processes produce unwanted by-products or are exces-
sively energy-consumptive, and there are many large-
scale conversions, such as alkylations, which use toxic
or corrosive materials. The results of the cumulative
investment in catalysis research are documented to a
large degree in the open and patent literature.1,2 Ex-
tensive practical knowledge is, however, also resident,
on a more restricted basis, within the R&D organiza-
tions of industrial catalysis practitioners.
In overviewing the application of combinatorial
methods to catalysis, we separate molecular catalysts
that are typically well-defined molecular entities, such
as used for hydroformylation, olefin metathesis, and
recent metallocene-based polymerizations, and hetero-
geneous catalysts, that are often complex, multiphasic,
with active sites that are dilute, and whose structures
are poorly understood at the atomic level.
MOLECULAR CATALYSIS
Molecular catalysts, either applied homogeneously, in
a gas phase or, more commonly, dissolved in a liquid
phase, or heterogeneously, in a supported or tethered
form, are defined as organic, organometallic, or inor-
ganic molecular compounds that (1) are synthesized as
discrete molecular entities, and (2) have molecular
structures that are well defined, even if not always
known in full detail. The practical catalyst may also
include cocatalyst, promoter, and support, any or all of
which might be structurally variable, complex, or ill-
defined at the molecular level. By this definition, mo-
lecular catalysts include enzymes, although enzyme
catalysis, being a field in its own right, is not consid-
ered further here. The remainder of molecular cataly-
sis represents some 15% of commercial catalyzed con-
versions,5 olefin polymerization and hydroformylation
being two of the largest, but with a suite of others that
include full and partial oxidations and reductions, ole-
fin metathesis, methanol carbonylation, CO2 hydroge-
nation and asymmetric synthesis.5–7
Combinatorial methods have been used to produce
catalysts for several target conversions, as well as to
optimize the conditions of catalyzed preparative reac-
tions; past overviews include references 8–12. In pro-
duction practice a heterogeneous format is usually pre-
ferred, and considerable effort is being invested to-
ward the “heterogenization” of homogeneous
Figure 1.
The performance of a given catalytic system represents the
combination of a set of properties. A given property is dependent on the
geometric structure of the total system, and of its components, both at the
atomic level (the ground-state electronic structure being defined by the
geometrical structure) and at longer length scales such as the mesoscopic.
Structure is controlled by the conditions of synthesis and processing.
204
BIOTECHNOLOGY AND BIOENGINEERING (COMBINATORIAL CHEMISTRY), VOL. 61, NO. 4, 1998/1999
